label: 193_lsap_lsaps_largescale asset_asset purchases
description: The Federal Reserve implemented multiple rounds of quantitative easing through large-scale asset purchases, including QE1, Operation Twist, and LSAPs. These measures aimed to lower long-term interest rates and stimulate the economy during the financial crisis.

earliest_articles.title: Large-scale asset purchases by the Federal Reserve: did they work?
earliest_articles.excerpt: Large-scale asset purchases by the Federal Reserve: did they work? Since December 2008, the Federal Reserve's traditional policy instrument, the target federal funds rate, has been effectively at its lower bound of zero. newest_articles.title: Open-Ended Treasury Purchases: From Market Functioning to Financial Easing
newest_articles.excerpt: We exploit the Fed’s Treasury purchases conducted from March 2020 to March 2022 to assess whether asset purchases can be tailored to accomplish different objectives: restoring market functioning and providing stimulus. We find that, on average, flow effects are significant in the market-functioning (MF) period…

newest_articles.excerpt: This post was most recently updated on January 27, 2022. central_articles.title: Operation Twist and the Effect of Large-Scale Asset Purchases
central_articles.excerpt: John F. Kennedy was elected president in November 1960 and inaugurated on January 20, 1961. The U.S. economy had been in recession for several months, so the incoming Administration and the Federal Reserve wanted to lower interest rates to stimulate the weak economy. However, Europe was not in a recession at the time…

central_articles.title: How Stimulatory Are Large-Scale Asset Purchases?
central_articles.excerpt: With the Federal Reserve’s benchmark federal funds rate near zero since late 2008, the central bank has used alternative tools to stimulate the economy. In particular, the Fed has purchased large quantities of long-term Treasury and mortgage-backed securities, a policy often referred to as quantitative easing. It has…
